# Transcrição: Curiosity_is_an_Important_Fuel_Source!

- **Fonte:** Curiosity_is_an_Important_Fuel_Source!
- **Duração:** 01:12
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 01:12]

I think curiosity can be a fuel source. I'm working with a group of mathematicians who are trying to inspire other teachers and students to become more curious about mathematics. Usually what happens is that your teacher will go, here's the formula, here's the problem, plug the problem into the formula. And people never get excited about maths. Whereas what they're trying to do now is they're trying to say, here's the problem. How do you think we can solve this? Like instead of giving them the formula straight away, they want to engage in their curiosity and go, what are the different ways in which you can solve this problem? When I was taught math when I was young is that there's only one way to solve that problem. You use this formula, it's the most efficient way to solve this problem. Here's the formula plug it in. Here's the formula plug it in. Here's the formula plug it in. There was really any thinking that was done. Whereas now they're trying to get kids to be more curious by saying, hey, here's the problem. How would you approach this? And that's such a better way of teaching because then it's teaching the kids that there are many ways to solve the same problem. You know, I see these posts on my Facebook or my Instagram of people who try something and they fail and then they just don't try again because in their own mind there's the undercurrent of, oh, there's only one way and if that way didn't work, there's no other way. Curiosity is very important. We've got to learn how to foster it in our own lives.
